_comment: Per-chart narratives. CI (build_prompt.py -> GitHub Models -> write_narrative.py) overwrites these from live numbers; this committed copy is the fallback so the page always reads well. source = 'fallback' | 'github-models'.

notes.fossil: As of June 2026, WTI crude trades at approximately $79.2 per barrel, Brent at $81.62, and US regular gasoline at $3.85 per gallon. Henry Hub natural gas is priced at $2.83 per MMBtu and is noted as the most volatile among these three fuels when indexed to 2019 (100). Natural gas experienced a significant spike in 2022 before retreating, whereas oil and gasoline prices moved more closely together. This volatility in natural gas prices reflects its sensitivity to market and supply dynamics more than crude oil or gasoline.
notes.transition: The levelised cost of electricity, which represents the average cost per megawatt-hour (MWh) over the lifetime of a power plant, shows significant advances in renewables. Solar PV has fallen to $44/MWh and onshore wind to $33/MWh, both now cheaper than new coal at $110/MWh and gas (CCGT) at $76/MWh. Offshore wind remains higher at $75/MWh. Solar PV's levelised cost has fallen about 90% since 2010, dropping from roughly $460/MWh to $44/MWh, underscoring strong improvements in technology and cost efficiency.
notes.currency: An estimated 20% of global oil trade is now settled outside the US dollar, a share that is rising but remains unofficial. Despite this shift, the US dollar continues to dominate global oil transactions. The estimated rise of non-dollar settlements is driven primarily by Russia's pivot towards Asian buyers and deals settled in yuan and rupees. This trend reflects evolving geopolitical and trade relationships rather than a fundamental displacement of the dollar's role.
notes.now: Current energy market dynamics display the interplay of fossil fuel pricing volatility, especially in natural gas, alongside a transition towards increasingly cost-competitive renewable electricity sources. Meanwhile, the global oil trade's currency composition is slowly diversifying, yet the US dollar maintains its central role. These factors collectively highlight both shifts and continuities in global energy economics as of mid-2026.
